title: Earth's Climate in Danger: UN Warns of Too Much Heat
summary: The United Nations says Earth's climate is in big trouble because it's getting too hot. This is caused by gases like carbon dioxide from burning coal and oil, which trap heat. Last year, the ocean got very warm and ice caps melted more. Scientists worry that a natural warm weather pattern called El Niño might make things even hotter soon. The UN leader, António Guterres, wants countries to stop using fossil fuels and switch to clean energy like solar and wind to keep everyone safe. He says every sign of climate change is warning us. The last 11 years have been the hottest ever recorded, and 2025 was 1.43 degrees Celsius warmer than before people used lots of fossil fuels. Even though a cool pattern called La Niña made 2025 a bit cooler, it was still one of the three hottest years. The Earth is taking in more heat energy than it can release, which is making the climate change fast.

keywords.explanation: the usual weather patterns on Earth
a gas from burning fuels that makes Earth warmer
a natural weather event that can make temperatures rise
things like coal and oil that we burn for energy
clean power from sources like sun and wind
when Earth gains more heat than it loses

questions.question: What is causing Earth to get too hot?
What natural event might make temperatures rise soon?
Who wants countries to use clean energy?
How many of the last years were the hottest on record?
What does 'energy imbalance' mean?
options: Earth gains more heat than it loses
Earth loses more heat than it gains
questions.correct_answer: Earth gains more heat than it loses

background_read: Climate change is when Earth's weather gets warmer over time because of human activities like burning fossil fuels. This can cause problems like melting ice, rising sea levels, and more extreme weather. The United Nations is an organization that helps countries work together on big issues like climate. El Niño and La Niña are natural patterns in the Pacific Ocean that affect weather around the world, with El Niño often bringing warmer temperatures. Switching to renewable energy helps reduce pollution and protect the planet for future generations.
Article_Structure: WHO: The United Nations and its weather agency, the World Meteorological Organization, along with UN leader António Guterres and scientists.
WHAT: Earth's climate is out of balance with record heat, caused by carbon dioxide from fossil fuels, leading to ocean warming and ice melt, and El Niño may worsen it.
WHERE: Globally, affecting the whole planet.
WHY: To warn people about climate dangers and urge action to switch to renewable energy for safety.

perspectives.description: The UN is alerting the world to urgent climate risks and calling for immediate changes to protect Earth.
